articles.title: UKOG sells off Horse Hill development in million pound deal
articles.summary: The London-listed firm has sold up to Energy B.
articles.body: UKOG sells off Horse Hill development in million pound deal The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Ryan Duff Aberdeen Features Lead In a deal worth over £1 million, UK Oil and Gas (UKOG) has sold off its stake in the Horse Hill oil field near Gatwick airport. The London-listed firm has agreed to hand its stake to Energy B in a deal which includes a share purchase agreement worth £1m. The buyer will takeover 100% of UKOG’s interest in the site as part of Energy B’s strategy to “build a portfolio of oil and gas projects in the UK in support of UK energy security”. David Lenigas, Energy B Chairman, added: “This is an incredibly exciting project and important for [the] future of UK energy sovereignty. “Not only is there a great deal of oil at Horse Hill, but there is also a lot of gas in this very live, shallow and extensive hydrocarbon system. That gas has historically been flared over the last decade, gas that could have been used to power or heat UK homes.” Energy B’s chief executive, Neil Ritson, also highlighted the importance of domestically produced hydrocarbons as he warned against the UK being too heavily reliant on imports. “The UK is on a path to net zero, however, we need to recognise that oil and gas will remain part of the energy mix for decades to come,” Ritson commented. “Importing foreign gas and oil; often with a much higher carbon footprint than indigenous supplies, is environmentally and economically unsound. “We hope to bring Horse Hill back on to production as soon as possible and to develop its greater potential as a springboard for further development of the Weald Basin as a source of indigenous energy.” In May, UKOG submitted an updated application to Surrey County Council (SCC) for work on the onshore oil field after a legal challenge blocked development. In 2024, climate campaigner Sarah Finch successfully challenged SCC’s approval of the UKOG project on the grounds that it did not consider downstream, or scope three, emissions from the site. Ultimately, the courts ruled in favour of Finch in June 2024 in a move which raised questions about how oil and gas projects would be consented both onshore and offshore across the UK. The verdict impacted the massive Rosebank field, which is the UK’s largest untapped oil field and had already received sign-off. The deal to sell Horse Hill comes as UKOG looks to diversify its holdings and pursue hydrogen storage opportunities. In March, UKOG’s wholly owned clean energy subsidiary, UK Energy Storage (UKEn), signed a deal with Wales and West Utilities to connect its South Dorset salt-cavern hydrogen storage site to a planned pipeline. And in the firm’s recently filed full-year report, chief executive Stephen Sanderson doubled down on hydrogen storage. Sanderson said: “Whilst the company recognises that potentially material resources likely remain within Horse Hill, this divestment presents a timely and attractive opportunity to complete UKOG’s exit from the UK onshore oil and gas sector, freeing our team and resources to focus upon our two material UK salt cavern energy storage projects and new international energy opportunities under active review. “We wish Energy B well in its future stewardship of Horse Hill and in realising its ambition to deliver the field’s full remaining potential.” The deal to acquire Horse Hill will also see Energy B take over 77.9% shareholding in Horse Hill Developments Ltd. Ryan Duff is Aberdeen features lead at Energy Voice. As well as reporting on the UK energy sector with a focus on skills and a just transition for the workforce, he also oversees the Energy Voice print edition and co-hosts the Out Loud by Energy Voice podcast. Got a story for Ryan? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

The London-listed firm has agreed to hand its stake to Energy B in a deal which includes a share purchase agreement worth £1m. The buyer will takeover 100% of UKOG’s interest in the site as part of Energy B’s strategy to “build a portfolio of oil and gas projects in the UK in support of UK energy security”. David Lenigas, Energy B Chairman, added: “This is an incredibly exciting project and important for [the] future of UK energy sovereignty. “Not only is there a great deal of oil at Horse Hill, but there is also a lot of gas in this very live, shallow and extensive hydrocarbon system. That gas has historically been flared over the last decade, gas that could have been used to power or heat UK homes.” Energy B’s chief executive, Neil Ritson, also highlighted the importance of domestically produced hydrocarbons as he warned against the UK being too heavily reliant on imports. “The UK is on a path to net zero, however, we need to recognise that oil and gas will remain part of the energy mix for decades to come,” Ritson commented. “Importing foreign gas and oil; often with a much higher carbon footprint than indigenous supplies, is environmentally and economically unsound. “We hope to bring Horse Hill back on to production as soon as possible and to develop its greater potential as a springboard for further development of the Weald Basin as a source of indigenous energy.” In May, UKOG submitted an updated application to Surrey County Council (SCC) for work on the onshore oil field after a legal challenge blocked development. In 2024, climate campaigner Sarah Finch successfully challenged SCC’s approval of the UKOG project on the grounds that it did not consider downstream, or scope three, emissions from the site. Ultimately, the courts ruled in favour of Finch in June 2024 in a move which raised questions about how oil and gas projects would be consented both onshore and offshore across the UK. The verdict impacted the massive Rosebank field, which is the UK’s largest untapped oil field and had already received sign-off. The deal to sell Horse Hill comes as UKOG looks to diversify its holdings and pursue hydrogen storage opportunities. In March, UKOG’s wholly owned clean energy subsidiary, UK Energy Storage (UKEn), signed a deal with Wales and West Utilities to connect its South Dorset salt-cavern hydrogen storage site to a planned pipeline. And in the firm’s recently filed full-year report, chief executive Stephen Sanderson doubled down on hydrogen storage. Sanderson said: “Whilst the company recognises that potentially material resources likely remain within Horse Hill, this divestment presents a timely and attractive opportunity to complete UKOG’s exit from the UK onshore oil and gas sector, freeing our team and resources to focus upon our two material UK salt cavern energy storage projects and new international energy opportunities under active review. “We wish Energy B well in its future stewardship of Horse Hill and in realising its ambition to deliver the field’s full remaining potential.” The deal to acquire Horse Hill will also see Energy B take over 77.9% shareholding in Horse Hill Developments Ltd. Ryan Duff is Aberdeen features lead at Energy Voice. As well as reporting on the UK energy sector with a focus on skills and a just transition for the workforce, he also oversees the Energy Voice print edition and co-hosts the Out Loud by Energy Voice podcast. Got a story for Ryan? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

articles.title: Archive pictures: Beryl field celebrates 50 years of production
articles.summary: It has been five decades since the world's first concrete deepwater platform produced its first barrel.
articles.body: Archive pictures: Beryl field celebrates 50 years of production The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Ryan Duff Aberdeen Features Lead The Beryl field more than doubled its initial operating time this week when it celebrated 50 years of production. The North Sea field, then owned by US supermajor ExxonMobil, started producing on 11 June 1976. At the time, the asset was forecast to produce for 25 years before decommissioning. However, following years of tieback activity, Beryl is still producing. The asset, which stands 100 miles off the east coast of Shetland, was initially discovered in 1972. By 1973, it was decided that the Beryl Alpha would be the world’s first concrete deepwater platform, and work began. Over its five decades of production, Beryl has expanded from being one platform operating one site. Indeed, the Greater Beryl Area has expanded over the past 50 years to encompass developments such as Nevis, Skene, Buckland and Lewis. In 2011, Apache acquired the Beryl field from ExxonMobil. Under Apache, cash was spent on redevelopment opportunities for the field, which included seismic work and drilling programmes. Read about the delivery of the Beryl Alpha platform here. “The Beryl Field holds a truly special place in both the history of the North Sea and the history of Apache,” said Greg McDaniel, senior vice president, International Assets. “Producing for 50 years is an incredible achievement and a testament to the strength of the asset and the people behind it. “The contribution to the UK in terms of energy security, jobs and economic value has been immense, and its legacy will endure for generations.” Over its five decades, the Beryl field has produced 1.14 billion barrels of oil and 3.8 trillion cubic feet of gas. Last year, Apache celebrated the 50th anniversary of production from another one of its North Sea titans. The Forties field, initially owned by BP, marked five decades of production in November 2025 . Since then, Apache has awarded Well-Safe Solutions a multi-year contract for decommissioning work on the Forties field. “When Apache acquired the Beryl, we recognised the significant opportunity that remained within the field,” added McDaniel. “Through investment, innovation and operational excellence, we continued to unlock value. “This milestone belongs to the people who have worked on the asset over the past five decades. Their commitment, professionalism and resilience have made Beryl one of the North Sea’s great success stories.” Beryl through the years Ryan Duff is Aberdeen features lead at Energy Voice. As well as reporting on the UK energy sector with a focus on skills and a just transition for the workforce, he also oversees the Energy Voice print edition and co-hosts the Out Loud by Energy Voice podcast. Got a story for Ryan? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

articles.title: Adura ‘could sue UK government’ if Rosebank and Jackdaw stopped
articles.summary: Aberdeen University Professor John Underhill raised the prospect of the Shell and Equinor-owned firm taking legal action if decision goes against them.
articles.body: Adura 'could sue UK government' if Rosebank and Jackdaw halted The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Erikka Askeland News Editor A North Sea oil gas company could consider legal action against the UK government if two key fields are not allowed to go ahead. Adura, which is leading the development of the controversial Rosebank and Jackdaw oil and gas fields, could sue the government to recover costs, a leading academic has warned. Aberdeen University professor John Underhill raised the prospect of legal action as the firm awaits permission from the government’s offshore oil and gas environmental regulator for the projects to proceed. Both of the multi-billion developments previously had their licences quashed by the courts over the emissions the oil and gas produced would create – known as scope 3 emissions. The judge ruled that the operators – then Shell and Equinor – could press ahead with development of the fields while the government considered new submissions on their environmental impact. Speaking at an event in Aberdeen, Underhill said Adura was nearly ready to start production with a platform in place for Jackdaw in the North Sea and the floating production storage and offloading (FPSO) unit for Rosebank nearly in place. He said the government’s decision whether or not to allow them to proceed, expected from the Offshore Petroleum Regulator for Environment and Decommissioning (OPRED), would be a “seismic event”. “It changes the dynamic if that permission is given. If permission is not given, do Adura sue for all the costs that they’ve had for Jackdaw and Rosebank? I think we’re in really interesting times,” he said to the audience at the European Association of Geoscientists and Engineers (EAGE) event in Aberdeen . Time is now ticking on the summer work season best suited to conducting work needed to complete development. However, the government has suggested the ball is in Adura’s court as it awaits further data. On his EAGE panel, Underwood joined other industry experts to discuss the challenges of meeting global energy demand, particularly amid the increased threat from the Middle East crisis. ‘We are down to a matter of weeks’ Meanwhile, Simon Flowers chairman and chief analyst for Wood Mackenzie, an energy analysis firm, told the audience he was “very worried” about the current state of the energy markets. He raised the prospect of oil reaching $150 per barrel, driving a global recession including in Europe and North America even if a resolution to the Iran conflict occurs quickly. “The reality is that we are running out of oil in some parts of the world. We’re down to a matter of weeks,” he said. He added: “Our analysis suggests that we’ll see Europe and the USA in recession, even if the price stays where it is today for the rest of the year. “A global recession is very much in prospect, so we fervently hope something is broken through and confines us all very, very soon. “Otherwise, it’s going to be very difficult territory into 2027.” In a statement, a spokesperson for the Department of Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) said the spike in oil and gas prices was evidence of why the UK “needs to get off the fossil fuel rollercoaster”. It said: “We are taking action to bear down on the cost of living and supporting de-escalation in the Middle East. “The lesson of yet another fossil fuel crisis is the UK needs to get off the fossil fuel rollercoaster and on to clean homegrown power we control.” A low emission solution? An Adura spokesperson said: “Adura is fully focused on supplying the information the regulator, OPRED, has requested to allow them to conclude assessment of the Rosebank and Jackdaw projects. “Approval of both projects would provide vital energy security for the UK as well as delivering economic benefits through direct investment, major supply chain contracts, increased tax revenues and support for thousands of well-paid jobs.” The firm added: “Jackdaw and Rosebank will be among the lowest emissions developments in the UK Continental Shelf (UKCS), itself one of the most highly-regulated and lowest emissions oil and gas basins anywhere in the world. “Across Adura’s portfolio of new developments – including Rosebank and Jackdaw – average production emissions intensity could be as much as half the UKCS average and around eight times lower than emissions intensity associated with imported LNG.” A continuing role for oil and gas Underhill, added that it is an “inconvenient reality” that the world and the UK largely still currently relies on oil and gas. “Globally we still rely on oil, gas, and coal for 80% or more of our total energy needs,” he said. “In the UK, it’s 75% that we rely on oil and gas, no longer coal, in order to meet the UK’s energy needs. “Whether we like it or not, that is not the perfect starting point for the transition. “We should not ignore the fact that oil and gas is right at the centre of this discussion. “We know where we want to go, but it’s going to take time, and the very word ‘transition’ in the dictionary says a translation from one state to another over time. “Many people wish and are portraying, including our politicians and others, that you can do this overnight. “I think that is fundamentally wrong in terms of the communication with the public. “We have to do better as a society, as a community, in communicating what we are trying to do and the role that oil and gas has and will continue to play.” Erikka Askeland is news editor at Energy Voice. She’s spent more than 20 years reporting and editing business news, with the last decade focused on the North Sea and the UK’s energy transition. Got a story for Erikka? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

He added: “Our analysis suggests that we’ll see Europe and the USA in recession, even if the price stays where it is today for the rest of the year. “A global recession is very much in prospect, so we fervently hope something is broken through and confines us all very, very soon. “Otherwise, it’s going to be very difficult territory into 2027.” In a statement, a spokesperson for the Department of Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) said the spike in oil and gas prices was evidence of why the UK “needs to get off the fossil fuel rollercoaster”. It said: “We are taking action to bear down on the cost of living and supporting de-escalation in the Middle East. “The lesson of yet another fossil fuel crisis is the UK needs to get off the fossil fuel rollercoaster and on to clean homegrown power we control.” A low emission solution? An Adura spokesperson said: “Adura is fully focused on supplying the information the regulator, OPRED, has requested to allow them to conclude assessment of the Rosebank and Jackdaw projects. “Approval of both projects would provide vital energy security for the UK as well as delivering economic benefits through direct investment, major supply chain contracts, increased tax revenues and su

rgy security for the UK as well as delivering economic benefits through direct investment, major supply chain contracts, increased tax revenues and support for thousands of well-paid jobs.” The firm added: “Jackdaw and Rosebank will be among the lowest emissions developments in the UK Continental Shelf (UKCS), itself one of the most highly-regulated and lowest emissions oil and gas basins anywhere in the world. “Across Adura’s portfolio of new developments – including Rosebank and Jackdaw – average production emissions intensity could be as much as half the UKCS average and around eight times lower than emissions intensity associated with imported LNG.” A continuing role for oil and gas Underhill, added that it is an “inconvenient reality” that the world and the UK largely still currently relies on oil and gas. “Globally we still rely on oil, gas, and coal for 80% or more of our total energy needs,” he said. “In the UK, it’s 75% that we rely on oil and gas, no longer coal, in order to meet the UK’s energy needs. “Whether we like it or not, that is not the perfect starting point for the transition. “We should not ignore the fact that oil and gas is right at the centre of this discussion. “We know where we want to go, but it’s going to take time, and the very word ‘transition’ in the dictionary says a translation from one state to another over time. “Many people wish and are portraying, including our politicians and others, that you can do this overnight.

ation from one state to another over time. “Many people wish and are portraying, including our politicians and others, that you can do this overnight. “I think that is fundamentally wrong in terms of the communication with the public. “We have to do better as a society, as a community, in communicating what we are trying to do and the role that oil and gas has and will continue to play.” Erikka Askeland is news editor at Energy Voice. She’s spent more than 20 years reporting and editing business news, with the last decade focused on the North Sea and the UK’s energy transition. Got a story for Erikka? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

articles.title: UK consortium plans Teesside direct air capture plant
articles.summary: Progressive Energy, Mission Zero Technologies, and Airhive combine to launch UnionDAC.
articles.body: UK consortium plans Teesside direct air capture plant The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Mathew Perry Reporter Three UK firms have announced plans to develop the country’s first commercial direct air capture (DAC) plant on Teesside. The UnionDAC project will connect to the Northern Endurance Partnership carbon capture and storage (CCS) project , with a target to start operations by 2032. The project is being developed as part of a collaboration between HyNet originators Progressive Energy and DAC technology firms Mission Zero Technologies and Airhive . The consortium said the UnionDAC development will “help build a new industrial future” on Teesside “support the region’s transition economy”. With the project progressing through the UK government’s East Coast Cluster expansion process , UnionDAC said it is aiming for a final investment decision in 2028. The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) confirmed between 1 and 2 million tonnes (Mt) of CO2 per year of transport and storage capacity will be available for allocation in this round. Once fully operational, the Teesside network is expected to be able to transport and permanently store up to 4Mt of CO₂ annually. The East Coast Cluster is one of two so-called ‘track-1’ CCS clusters selected by the UK government, alongside HyNet in the North West. A similar expansion round connected to HyNet attracted bids from Swiss DAC firm Climeworks, Uniper, EET Fuels, Progressive Energy, Evero Energy and Enfinium . UnionDAC said the Teesside project uses a modular approach designed to support future large-scale deployment in the UK and internationally. The Tees Valley Combined Authority said the plant will be located at the Wilton International Site in Teesworks, and capture 60,000 tonnes of CO₂ annually by 2032. What is direct air capture? DAC technology involves directly capturing carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, as opposed to directly sequestering industrial emissions. Swiss firm Climeworks has put forward its Silver Birch project as potentially the UK’s first large-scale DAC plant. Climeworks, which o perates the world’s largest operational DAC facility in Iceland , pointed to estimates suggesting Britain may need up to 17 million tonnes of DAC capacity by 2050. It comes as governments worldwide have invested nearly $4 billion in DAC development since 2020 , according to the International Energy Agency. But the technology’s track record has raised questions. While Climeworks has raised more than $800 million from investors, it has struggled to meet removal targets at its plants in Iceland and cut 20% of its workforce last year. The firm’s struggles have raised questions over the viability of direct carbon removal (DCR) technologies amid ongoing debate over their climate credentials . Mission Zero Technologies London-based Mission Zero Technologies is among the UK firms aiming to ensure captured carbon can be utilised cost-effectively. Founded by materials scientists, the company has developed an electrochemical DAC technology which can easily integrate with intermittent renewables and take advantage of curtailed wind power. Mission Zero has already piloted projects in Sheffield, where captured CO2 is used to make sustainable aviation fuel (SAF). Another Mission Zero project in Norfolk produces carbon-negative building materials, while a larger venture in Alberta with Canadian firm Deep Sky is also underway . Commenting on the UnionDAC project, Mission Zero said it is providing modular electrochemical technology to deliver 30,000 tonnes of annual capture capacity. “This project is a landmark blueprint for scaling British 🇬🇧 innovation, driving carbon removal, and bringing valuable investment to the Tees region,” the company said. “It’s a critical step toward being able to fulfil national Carbon Budgets and positioning the UK as a global cleantech leader.” Airhive Fellow London-based firm Airhive is also a partner in the Deep Sky Alpha project in Canada. The company has developed a DAC system based on fluidised bed technology and calcium-based sorbents. The three-step process uses natural minerals that chemically bind the carbon dioxide from the air, then unbinds it in a pure form for sequestration or utilisation. Airhive said the UnionDAC project is set to be “one of the largest and most advanced DAC systems anywhere in the world”. Carbon removal risks and outlook Despite growing interest in DCR technologies, major hurdles remain. Monitoring and verification standards remain unproven, while voluntary carbon markets are volatile. Globally, investment in carbon dioxide removal research and start-ups accounts for just 1.1% of overall investment in climate-tech start-ups . This is despite estimates that the world will need to remove between 7 and 9 billion tonnes of CO2 per year by 2050 to keep Paris climate goals within reach. Investment in carbon dioxide removal peaked in 2022, at around $1.5bn, before declining to $856m in 2023 according to a 2024 University of Oxford-led report . Deployment is still tiny, with around 20 DAC plants capturing only roughly 10,000 tonnes of CO2 per year. The UK’s Climate Change Committee says the country may need up to 23m tonnes annually by 2050 , with the Humber, Teesside, Merseyside and Grangemouth listed as prime candidates for deployment. Meanwhile the IEA’s net zero scenario calls for 85m tonnes globally by 2030 and nearly 1Gt by mid-century. Mathew Perry is a Glasgow-based reporter for Energy Voice. He covers the UK’s energy transition, with a focus on Scotland and the North Sea. Got a story for Mathew? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

articles.title: UK tops offshore wind injury list as safety rates improve
articles.summary: Lost time injury rates fell in UK despite a high overall count, according to offshore wind health and safety body G+.
articles.body: UK tops offshore wind injury list as safety rates improve The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Mathew Perry Reporter The UK recorded more offshore wind safety injuries than any other country in 2025, although injury rates across the sector fell, according to a report. In its 2025 incident data report, offshore wind health and safety body G+ found the UK logged 246 injuries during the year, the most of any reporting country, across 24.2 million work hours. Despite the high injury count, UK safety rates improved overall compared to the 2024 report . The report found the UK total recordable injury rate (TRIR) fell 8% to 3.02, while the lost time injury frequency (LTIF) fell 19% to 1.36. Both figures are measured per million hours worked. Injury rates at UK construction site rates improved, with TRIR falling from 2.76 to 1.49 and LTIF from 1.46 to 0.91. However, injury rates at operational UK wind farms worsened, with TRIR rising from 4.59 to 6.00, although LTIF eased from 2.55 to 2.24. Turbine nacelles became the largest source of injuries in the UK in 2025, with a 140% increase from 20 incidents in 2024 to 48 in 2025. G+ said the rise reflected sites completing construction and moving into operation, where technicians spend more time inside nacelles carrying out routine maintenance. Global offshore wind safety Globally, G+ recorded 1,791 total reports and 786 injuries across 2025 over 69.2 million work hours, with work hours up 5% on 2024. The global TRIR rose 4% to 3.48, while LTIF fell 8% to 1.34. No fatalities were recorded among G+ members in 2025, the first year without a member fatality since 2022. However, there were 51 injuries that resulted in an emergency response or medical evacuation (ERME), up by 6% compared to 2024. The report also listed three fatal incidents in the wider industry that fell outside its reporting scope. These involved a worker knocked overboard during survey operations, a crew member who died during vessel maintenance, and a worker who fell into the sea during a transfer at a construction site. The turbine nacelle was the largest incident area globally, with 114 injuries, more than double the 56 recorded in 2024. Manual handling remained the work process responsible for the most injuries, with 121, while routine maintenance injuries more than doubled, rising 146% to 91. High potential reports, those with the greatest likelihood of causing serious harm, fell 14% to 211, representing 12% of the total. Wind sector shows ‘encouraging progress’, G+ says G+ chair and Ørsted senior vice president Lisbeth Frømling said the data showed encouraging progress but also pointed to where attention was needed. “As offshore wind continues to scale globally, maintaining a strong safety performance depends on how effectively we respond to an evolving risk landscape,” Frømling said. “This year’s data demonstrates encouraging progress but also underlines the need to focus on operational safety, particularly in routine activities and manual handling.” Based at the Energy Institute in London, G+ members include major global offshore wind operators and wind turbine manufacturers including Jera Nex BP, Equinor, TotalEnergies, SSE and Siemens Gamesa among others. Energy Institute chief executive Dr Nick Wayth said the sector was improving in key areas but also changing as projects increasingly move past the construction phase. “As offshore wind moves into an increasingly operational phase, managing everyday risks becomes critical,” Wayth said. “A secure energy transition depends on the safety of the people behind it, and the Energy Institute is committed to supporting the sector in turning insight into safer outcomes on the ground.” Offshore wind safety concerns The figures follow sustained concerns from unions and researchers over safety in the offshore wind sector. A University of Strathclyde study published in 2024 found the sector’s injury rate was four times higher than offshore oil and gas . The study likened the UK offshore wind industry to the North Sea oil and gas sector during its rapid growth in the 1970s. The Rail, Maritime and Transport union has said the low number of dedicated offshore wind inspectors in the UK shows the need for better regulation , while the GMB union has urged the sector to adopt safety frameworks developed in oil and gas. Looking ahead to 2026, G+ said it will update its data collection to “have a closer look at injury severity, as well as incident causes”. Mathew Perry is a Glasgow-based reporter for Energy Voice. He covers the UK’s energy transition, with a focus on Scotland and the North Sea. Got a story for Mathew? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

Wind sector shows ‘encouraging progress’, G+ says G+ chair and Ørsted senior vice president Lisbeth Frømling said the data showed encouraging progress but also pointed to where attention was needed. “As offshore wind continues to scale globally, maintaining a strong safety performance depends on how effectively we respond to an evolving risk landscape,” Frømling said. “This year’s data demonstrates encouraging progress but also underlines the need to focus on operational safety, particularly in routine activities and manual handling.” Based at the Energy Institute in London, G+ members include major global offshore wind operators and wind turbine manufacturers including Jera Nex BP, Equinor, TotalEnergies, SSE and Siemens Gamesa among others. Energy Institute chief executive Dr Nick Wayth said the sector was improving in key areas but also changing as projects increasingly move past the construction phase. “As offshore wind moves into an increasingly operational phase, managing everyday risks becomes critical,” Wayth said. “A secure energy transition depends on the safety of the people behind it, and the Energy Institute is committed to supporting the sector in turning insight into safer outcomes on the ground.” Offshore wind safety concerns The figures follow sustained concerns from unions and researchers over safety in the offshore wind sector. A University of Strathclyde study published in 2024 found the sector’s injury rate was four times higher than offshore oil and gas . The study likened the UK offshore wind industry to the North Sea oil and gas sector during its rapid growth in the 1970s. The Rail, Maritime and Transport union has said the low number of dedicated offshore wind inspectors in the UK shows the need for better regulation , while the GMB union has urged the sector to adopt safety frameworks developed in oil and gas. Looking ahead to 2026, G+ said it will update its data collection to “have a closer look at injury severity, as well as incident causes”. Mathew Perry is a Glasgow-based reporter for Energy Voice. He covers the UK’s energy transition, with a focus on Scotland and the North Sea. Got a story for Mathew? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking
chunks: UK tops offshore wind injury list as safety rates improve. UK tops offshore wind injury list as safety rates improve The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Mathew Perry Reporter The UK recorded more offshore wind safety injuries than any other country in 2025, although injury rates across the sector fell, according to a report. In its 2025 incident data report, offshore wind health and safety body G+ found the UK logged 246 injuries during the year, the most of any reporting country, across 24.2 million work hours. Despite the high injury count, UK safety rates improved overall compared to the 2024 report . The report found the UK total recordable injury rate (TRIR) fell 8% to 3.02, while the lost time injury frequency (LTIF) fell 19% to 1.36. Both figures are measured per million hours worked. Injury rates at UK construction site rates improved, with TRIR falling from 2.76 to 1.49 and LTIF from 1.46 to 0.91. However, injury rates at operational UK wind farms worsened, with TRIR rising from 4.59 to 6.00, although LTIF eased from 2.55 to 2.24. Turbine nacelles became the largest source of injuries in the UK in 2025, with a 140% increase from 20 incidents in 2024 to 48 in 2025. G+ said the rise reflected sites completing construction and moving into operation, where technicians spend more time inside nacelles carrying out routine maintenance.

reflected sites completing construction and moving into operation, where technicians spend more time inside nacelles carrying out routine maintenance. Global offshore wind safety Globally, G+ recorded 1,791 total reports and 786 injuries across 2025 over 69.2 million work hours, with work hours up 5% on 2024. The global TRIR rose 4% to 3.48, while LTIF fell 8% to 1.34. No fatalities were recorded among G+ members in 2025, the first year without a member fatality since 2022. However, there were 51 injuries that resulted in an emergency response or medical evacuation (ERME), up by 6% compared to 2024. The report also listed three fatal incidents in the wider industry that fell outside its reporting scope. These involved a worker knocked overboard during survey operations, a crew member who died during vessel maintenance, and a worker who fell into the sea during a transfer at a construction site. The turbine nacelle was the largest incident area globally, with 114 injuries, more than double the 56 recorded in 2024. Manual handling remained the work process responsible for the most injuries, with 121, while routine maintenance injuries more than doubled, rising 146% to 91. High potential reports, those with the greatest likelihood of causing serious harm, fell 14% to 211, representing 12% of the total.

articles.title: ‘No time to spare’: UK must act now to double offshore wind workforce, report warns
articles.summary: ORE Catapult report finds the UK will need to grow offshore wind employment toward 94,000 by 2030.
articles.body: UK must act now to double offshore wind workforce, report warns The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Mathew Perry Reporter The UK must almost double its offshore wind workforce to as many as 94,000 people by 2030 to hit clean power targets, a report from ORE Catapult has warned. The report said the sector currently employed around 40,000 people, up from 32,000 in 2023. ORE Catapult said meeting the government’s Clean Power 2030 ambition of 43 to 50 GW of offshore wind would require the workforce to grow to between 75,000 and 94,000. These employment figures were first set out in the 2025 Wind Industry Skills Intelligence Report by RenewableUK and the Offshore Wind Industry Council. The report consolidates findings from five linked studies carried out between 2023 and 2025, drawing on input from over 60 participants across 46 organisations. UK skills training struggling to keep pace ORE Catapult said the UK’s education and training systems often struggled to keep pace with rapidly evolving technologies. The organisation called for a shift from a “lagging” model, in which training responds to existing demand, to a “leading” model that anticipates future skills needs. To deliver this, the report proposed grouping future energy roles into a set of role archetypes. This would allow educators to focus on new and emerging capabilities without redesigning entire courses for each role, ORE Catapult said. Key roles identified include wind turbine technicians, high voltage cable specialists, installation engineers, fabrication specialists, planning officers, technical managers, cable and design engineers, and electrical managers. No time to spare, ORE Catapult says ORE Catapult future skills manager Danielle Portsmouth said that without a clear focus on increasing the pipeline of skills and talent into the sector, the UK would not be able to maintain its position as a global leader in offshore wind. “Without immediate action, the capabilities and capacity of our current workforce will be insufficient to achieve the UK’s 2030 targets in offshore wind,” Portsmouth said. “There is little, if any, time to spare.” Portsmouth said the full cycle of developing course content, recruiting learners, re-skilling and giving new employees on-the-job experience would take until 2030 if started now. RenewableUK skills and social value manager James Lord said the UK needed to ensure the offshore wind workforce has the right skills and capabilities as technology evolves. “This means that people entering the sector need the training and skills to fit the highly specialised jobs which will be on offer when they step into the workforce in the years ahead,” Lord said. “This report identifies a series of vital measures which will enable us to fill specific key roles with staff who have appropriate levels of expertise and experience to achieve this, creating tens of thousands of high-quality well-paid jobs throughout the UK.” He reiterated that the report showed there is “no time to lose” if the workforce was to be ready to deliver the pipeline of projects needed to meet the UK’s offshore wind targets. Offshore wind skills recommendations Among the key recommendations in the report , ORE Catapult called for working groups to be established across education and the offshore wind sector. It also recommended working with industry partners on initiatives including an MSc in renewable energy, and extending ORE Catapult’s foresighting work through an online data tool. The organisation also called for the appointment of sector champions for five emerging technology areas. These five technology areas include dynamic cable systems, remote and autonomous systems for operations and maintenance, automated welding for offshore wind flotation structures, advanced manufacture of wind turbine blades, and UK production of high voltage direct current (HVDC) cable systems. The government has previously committed more than £100 million over three years to support engineering skills development for the sector. In addition, the pilot stage of an initiative to allow offshore oil and gas workers to more easily transition to roles in renewables launched in 2025 . The ORE Catapult findings follow the launch of the Scottish government’s first dedicated offshore wind skills plan in November last year . The plan set out 12 initial actions to train the specialist electricians, engineers and technicians needed to expand the sector. Mathew Perry is a Glasgow-based reporter for Energy Voice. He covers the UK’s energy transition, with a focus on Scotland and the North Sea. Got a story for Mathew? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

articles.title: 13 of the best pictures as EAGE comes to Aberdeen
articles.summary: The Granite City hosted thousands of delegates at the European Association of Geoscientists and Engineers event - and Energy Voice was there to capture all the action.
articles.body: 13 of the best pictures as EAGE comes to Aberdeen The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Dave Lord A major conference in Aberdeen has been rocking the world of geoscientists. The annual EAGE event was staged at P&J Live – offering thousands of delegates the chance to network, make connections and take part in a series of workshops and discussions. Energy Voice was in situ throughout the four-day conference – take a peek here to find out what our team made of it all (with apologies for the appalling puns): Among those taking part in the event was BP’s subsurface boss Ariel Flores , who said stable energy policies could pave the way to billions of pounds of investment across the globe. Other big players included the NSTA, who told Energy Voice they were keen to “maximise the use of existing infrastructure” in the North Sea. Across a particularly colourful show floor there was plenty to keep delegates interested. Energy Voice photographer Eve Conroy was there to capture the action: Dave Lord is national editorial director for Energy Voice and E-FWD . He’s spent over 25 years in journalism, telling stories that inform, challenge, and spark discussion. Got a story for Dave? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

articles.title: Serica Energy completes purchase of ONE-Dyas North Sea assets
articles.summary: The deal includes a 10% interest in the Catcher Field and a 5.21% interest in the Golden Eagle Area.
articles.body: Serica Energy completes purchase of ONE-Dyas North Sea assets The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Mathew Perry Reporter Serica Energy has completed its acquisition of two Central North Sea producing interests from ONE-Dyas. First announced in September last year, Serica has settled the $6.75 million consideration for a 10% interest in the Catcher Field and a 5.21% interest in the Golden Eagle Area Development (GEAD). The Catcher Field is operated by Harbour Energy, while CNOOC operates the GEAD. The operator has also received a payment of $13m reflecting interim post-tax cashflows between the economic date of 1 January 2024 and completion. Serica said the completed deal adds current net production of around 2,500 barrels of oil equivalent per day (boepd). It also includes combined net proved and probable (2P) reserves of 3 million boe and contingent (2C) resources of 500,000 boe, both as at 31 December 2025. In addition, Serica will receive around 85,000 boe in respect of an underlift position, with the associated cash proceeds of around $8m be received in Q3. Serica Energy expansion The deal for the ONE-Dyas assets came as part of a wider series of deals announced by Serica on 30 September 2025 . Serica acquired Prax Upstream, the collapsed upstream arm of fuel group Prax , for $25.6m. Prax Upstream had agreed to purchase the ONE-Dyas interests as part of a broader package of North Sea assets, and those agreements transferred to Serica as part of the corporate acquisition. The September 2025 package also included a 40% operated interest in the Greater Laggan Area and the associated Shetland Gas Plant, which Serica acquired from TotalEnergies. In recent months, Serica has also agreed to acquire Centrica’s remaining North Sea producing stakes in a £57m deal. The Spirit Energy deal covered interests in the Cygnus field and the Greater Markham Area in the Southern North Sea. Mathew Perry is a Glasgow-based reporter for Energy Voice. He covers the UK’s energy transition, with a focus on Scotland and the North Sea. Got a story for Mathew? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

The Catcher Field is operated by Harbour Energy, while CNOOC operates the GEAD. The operator has also received a payment of $13m reflecting interim post-tax cashflows between the economic date of 1 January 2024 and completion. Serica said the completed deal adds current net production of around 2,500 barrels of oil equivalent per day (boepd). It also includes combined net proved and probable (2P) reserves of 3 million boe and contingent (2C) resources of 500,000 boe, both as at 31 December 2025. In addition, Serica will receive around 85,000 boe in respect of an underlift position, with the associated cash proceeds of around $8m be received in Q3. Serica Energy expansion The deal for the ONE-Dyas assets came as part of a wider series of deals announced by Serica on 30 September 2025 . Serica acquired Prax Upstream, the collapsed upstream arm of fuel group Prax , for $25.6m.

series of deals announced by Serica on 30 September 2025 . Serica acquired Prax Upstream, the collapsed upstream arm of fuel group Prax , for $25.6m. Prax Upstream had agreed to purchase the ONE-Dyas interests as part of a broader package of North Sea assets, and those agreements transferred to Serica as part of the corporate acquisition. The September 2025 package also included a 40% operated interest in the Greater Laggan Area and the associated Shetland Gas Plant, which Serica acquired from TotalEnergies. In recent months, Serica has also agreed to acquire Centrica’s remaining North Sea producing stakes in a £57m deal. The Spirit Energy deal covered interests in the Cygnus field and the Greater Markham Area in the Southern North Sea. Mathew Perry is a Glasgow-based reporter for Energy Voice. He covers the UK’s energy transition, with a focus on Scotland and the North Sea. Got a story for Mathew? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

articles.title: Motive Offshore reels in subsea cable firm, C-Kore
articles.summary: Boyndie-based Motive said bringing in C-Kore's subsea testing and measurement tools introduces 'a technology-driven focus' for the firm.
articles.body: Motive Offshore reels in subsea cable firm, C-Kore The first word in energy Be the first to know with in-app alerts and exclusive reporting Erikka Askeland News Editor Motive Offshore Group ( Motive ) has announced the acquisition of subsea cable monitoring C-Kore to boost its testing, inspection and certification (TIC) capabilities. The acquisition of York-based C-Kore is expected to increase Motive’s certification and inspection division to approximately 35% of total turnover, around £30 million, the firm said. It added the deal represents a “development in the company’s strategic direction by introducing a technology-driven focus, dedicated R&D and proprietary IP”. C-Kore was launched in 2012 by founding chairman Tim Overfield, 75. It was developed as a measurement tool through his subsea control firm Zetechtics. Both firms were recognised with a King’s Award for Enterprise – international trade in 2024 and C-Kore again in 2026. C-Kore’s suite of subsea testing and measurement tools provide rapid automated testing for fault detection and installation operations, resulting in a reduction in both vessel and personnel costs. Motive said the tools also have potential to expand into domestic and international offshore wind and decommissioning markets. C-Kore will continue to be led within Motive by existing operations director, Greg Smith and sales and marketing director, Cynthia Pikaar, from its Yorkshire headquarters. The firm employs around eight people, according to its most recent accounts. Motive CEO, Dave Acton, said: “C‑Kore’s ability to win and execute global projects through a single UK entity has been impressive. We see a real opportunity to scale the success already achieved by Greg, Cynthia and the wider team, whilst providing enhanced customer support, via Motive’s regional entities across the Americas, Europe, Middle East and Asia-Pacific . “C-Kore’s proven, technology-led capabilities not only complement our subsea testing and inspection services by aligning with the developing requirements of our existing client base, but also underline our objective to provide integrated and highly transferable equipment, services and technology across the energy sector.” C-Kore’s Smith said Motive’s “global reach and strong market presence will bring our award-winning technology to a wider customer base and accelerate our move into new markets worldwide”. He added: “We’re incredibly proud of the innovative and sophisticated cost-saving technology the C-Kore team has delivered so far. Uniting with Motive allows us to build on our achievements while continuing to support our existing customers without interruption.” Erikka Askeland is news editor at Energy Voice. She’s spent more than 20 years reporting and editing business news, with the last decade focused on the North Sea and the UK’s energy transition. Got a story for Erikka? Get in touch here . Tags Breaking

articles.title: Dangote ramp-up slashes WAF product imports, reshapes clean tanker routes: analysts
articles.summary: The ramp-up of Nigeria’s 650,000 b/d refinery has cut imports of clean products to West Africa and is redirecting tanker flows around the region, according to analysts. West African imports of clean refined products fell to 765,000 b/d in May from 997,000 b/d in April, a 23% decline, S&P Global Commodities at Sea(opens in a ...
articles.body: Dangote ramp-up slashes WAF product imports, reshapes clean tanker routes: analysts | Hellenic Shipping News Worldwide Recent News Why the economics make this the craziest World Cup ever Dangote ramp-up slashes WAF product imports, reshapes clean tanker routes: analysts Tanker Market Stays Elevated in May Trade stands at a crossroads Retrofitting at scale: Lessons from delivering the world’s first methanol mega containership conversion Dangote ramp-up slashes WAF product imports, reshapes clean tanker routes: analysts in International Shipping News 13/06/2026 The ramp-up of Nigeria’s 650,000 b/d refinery has cut imports of clean products to West Africa and is redirecting tanker flows around the region, according to analysts. West African imports of clean refined products fell to 765,000 b/d in May from 997,000 b/d in April, a 23% decline, S&P Global Commodities at Sea(opens in a new tab) data showed June 10. Analysts at shipping association BIMCO reported a higher decrease of 44% over the same timeframe. The drop in import volumes cut ton-miles by 47% year-over-year, lowering the region’s share of global CPP ton-miles from 5.3% to 3.6%, BIMCO said in a June 10 research note. “LR1 and LR2 product tankers recorded the largest declines, down 88% and 78% respectively, and together accounted for 55% of the total ton-miles loss,” BIMCO said. MR product tanker ton-miles fell only 4% year over year during April to May, despite a sharp drop in imports from all major loading regions. A 34-fold increase in volumes from the Americas largely offset the decline, limiting the overall loss, BIMCO said. Historically, the Rotterdam-to-Lagos route was a cornerstone of the MR tanker market; as the Dangote refinery met over 90% of Nigeria’s domestic demand by early 2026, these imports plummeted, analysts at CAS said May 15. “Data indicated a 39% year-on-year drop in Nigerian CPP imports by mid-2025, essentially removing a massive source of employment for MR tankers in the Atlantic,” the CAS analysts said. Platts, part of S&P Global Energy, assessed the rate to carry a 37,000 metric ton cargo of clean products from the UK/Continent to West Africa at an average of $27.95/mt in June 2025, down 14% from June 2024. The rate averaged $36.19/mt June 1-9, 2026, amid wider freight rate support from disruption in the Middle East. Dangote was commissioned in 2024 and hit its 650,000 b/d capacity for the first time in February 2026. According to government figures, the refinery supplied roughly 80% of Nigeria’s gasoline – its main fuel market – in April. After increasing its capacity to 700,000 b/d, Dangote says it can serve 150% of recent consumption levels, albeit supported by higher imports of gasoline blendstock from mostly European countries. New role The slide in Western Africa’s imports appears to reflect a reduced role for Lome, Togo, as a key offshore storage and redistribution hub. Imports into Lome offshore storage, and re-exports from there to Western Africa, saw a larger volume decline than the region overall, Niels Rasmussen, chief shipping analyst at BIMCO, said in the research note. “As Lome may never regain Nigeria’s volumes, its position as a major offshore import and storage hub could be lost along with the related tanker demand. It could, however, remain an important offshore distribution hub for smaller markets and ports,” Rasmussen said. Clean products are now being shipped from Lagos to West African neighbors, such as Ghana, Togo, Ivory Coast, and further afield to South Korea, the US, and Europe. This shift is replacing long-haul imports with shorter, regional “shuttle” voyages, which increase the frequency of tanker calls but reduce overall tonne-miles compared to global imports, CAS analysts said. The refinery’s export strategy has increased demand for LR1 and LR2 tankers capable of moving large product volumes to international hubs like Singapore or Rotterdam, CAS analysts said. “This transition from being a destination for MRs to a source for LRs is reshaping the global fleet distribution,” they said. The loss of the lucrative Europe-to-Nigeria gasoline run has created a surplus of MR tankers in the Atlantic Basin, CAS analysts said. While this would typically depress freight rates, its effect has been mitigated by the refinery’s own export volumes and the need for tankers to service new regional hubs, CAS analysts said. Dangote has emerged as a growing export hub in the wake of the Middle East conflict, shipping a record 372,000 b/d of clean petroleum products in April, according to CAS data. New capacities With its planned expansion to double capacity by 2028, Dangote aims to offset even greater volumes of imports that once flowed into the West African market and to cement its role as a global export hub. After hitting constraints with its single-point mooring system, the company is also building a new marine jetty to support smaller shipments of LR2 size and under into regional markets like Namibia, where it is constructing a 240-million barrel tank farm. The fully automated systems at Lekki Deep Sea Port—including automated berthing and digital clearance through its computerized terminal operating system—are designed to reduce cargo wait times from over 50 days to just two days. “For larger LR2 tankers, this precision significantly slashes vessel turnaround times, minimizing port dwell time and the accrued expenses of demurrage, which is critical for maintaining the high-velocity export schedule required by the refinery,” CAS analysts said. Source: Platts 2026-06-13 hellenicshippingnews... The fully automated systems at Lekki Deep Sea Port—including automated berthing and digital clearance through its computerized terminal operating system—are designed to reduce cargo wait times from over 50 days to just two days. “For larger LR2 tankers, this precision significantly slashes vessel turnaround times, minimizing port dwell time and the accrued expenses of demurrage, which is critical for maintaining the high-velocity export schedule required by the refinery,” CAS analysts said. articles.title: Tanker Market Stays Elevated in May
articles.summary: Dirty tanker spot freight rates remained elevated in May, although down from the record highs registered in March, OPEC said in its latest monthly report. Increased long-haul demand supported VLCC spot freight rates. The repositioning of tankers made more available in the Atlantic Basin, easing upward pressure on rates. On the West Africa-to-East route, VLCC ...
articles.body: Tanker Market Stays Elevated in May | Hellenic Shipping News Worldwide Recent News Why the economics make this the craziest World Cup ever Dangote ramp-up slashes WAF product imports, reshapes clean tanker routes: analysts Tanker Market Stays Elevated in May Trade stands at a crossroads Retrofitting at scale: Lessons from delivering the world’s first methanol mega containership conversion Tanker Market Stays Elevated in May in Hellenic Shipping News 13/06/2026 D irty tanker spot freight rates remained elevated in May, although down from the record highs registered in March, OPEC said in its latest monthly report. Increased long-haul demand supported VLCC spot freight rates. The repositioning of tankers made more available in the Atlantic Basin, easing upward pressure on rates. On the West Africa-to-East route, VLCC spot freight rates edged 4% lower, m-o-m, but remained 121% higher than in the same month last year. Suezmax spot freight rates followed a similar dynamic, despite US Gulf crude exports remaining near record levels amid ongoing SPR releases. On the USGC-to-Europe route, rates fell 22%, m-o-m, but were 130% higher, y-o-y. Aframax spot freight rates experienced the largest drop but still stood at the upper end of the five-year range. Rates on the Mediterranean-to-Northwest Europe route fell 51%, m-o-m, amid reduced prompt buying following strong levels in the previous month. Clean tanker spot freight rates remained elevated East of Suez on Asian demand, but declined West of Suez as prompt buying eased amid ample trade flows ahead of seasonal demand. Clean spot freight rates on the Middle East-to-East route were up 9%, m-o-m, while rates around the Mediterranean fell 32%, m-o-m. Dirty tanker freight rates Very large crude carriers (VLCC) VLCC spot freight rates fell further in May but remained well above the five-year average. Rates were supported by increasing long-haul demand, particularly for Atlantic Basin crudes to Asia to address ongoing supply disruptions. On average, VLCC spot freight rates were up 5%, m-o-m, and 520%, y-o-y. On the Middle East-to-East route, rates were assessed at WS587 in May. This represents a m-o-m increase of 7% and an 878% jump compared to the same month last year. On the Middle East-to-West route, spot freight rates were assessed at WS224. This indicates a rise of 6%, m-o-m, and 579%, y-o-y. On the more active West Africa-to-East route, spot freight rates fell 4% to average WS137. The decline came amid continued repositioning from Asia to the more active Atlantic Basin. Compared with the same month in 2025, rates were up 121%. Suezmax Suezmax spot freight rates followed a similar dynamic, despite US Gulf crude exports remaining near record levels amid ongoing SPR releases. On average, Suezmax rates were down 21%, m-o-m, but remained 129% higher than in the same month last year. On the West Africa-to-USGC route, spot freight rates in May averaged WS183, down 19%, m-o-m, but 129% higher than the same month last year. Rates on the USGC-to-Europe route fell 22%, m-o-m, to average WS170. This was despite estimates showing that US crude exports remained near record levels of over 5 mb/d. Y-o-y, however, rates were 130% higher. Aframax Aframax spot freight rates experienced the largest drop but still stood at the upper end of the five-year range. M-o-m, rates were down by an average of 40%. Declines were softer East of Suez, where supply concerns were more persistent. Compared with the same month in 2025, Aframax rates were still elevated, averaging 76% higher. Spot freight rates on the Indonesia-to-East route fell 16%, m-o-m, to average WS221. Y-o-y, rates on the route were up by 91%. On the Caribbean-to-US East Coast (USEC) route, rates fell 44%, m-o-m, to average W299. Compared with the same month last year, rates were up 121%. Cross-Med rates registered sharper declines, falling 40%, m-o-m, to average WS204. Compared to the same month of 2025, spot rates on the route were still up by 48%. Rates on the Med-to-Northwest Europe (NWE) route were down 51%, m-o-m, to average WS197. Compared with the same month in 2025, rates were 44% higher. Clean tanker freight rates In contrast to the dirty tanker market, clean spot freight rates showed divergent trends East and West of Suez. The East of Suez market managed to register a 4% gain, m-o-m, based on assessments of rates on routes to Asia out of the Middle East. In the West of Suez, rates declined by 31%, m-o-m, as prompt buying eased. Compared with the same month in 2025, Aframax rates remained elevated, averaging 132% above year-ago levels. Rates on the Middle East-to-East route were assessed at WS596 on average, up 9%, m-o-m, the only gain on monitored routes. Compared with May 2025, rates were 292% higher. Clean spot freight rates on the Singapore-to-East route dipped 5%, m-o-m, to average WS302. Y-o-y, rates on the route were up 91%. East of Suez routes showed a stronger decline, m-o-m, with rates on the NWE-to-USEC route down 29% to average WS195. This was still an increase of 47%, y-o-y. Rates on both the Cross-Med and Med-to-NEW routes fell 32%, m-o-m, to average WS349 and WS359, respectively. Y-o-y, spot freight rates were up 115% on the Cross-Med route and up 109% on the Med-to-NWE route. Nikos Roussanoglou, Hellenic Shipping News Worldwide Tops 2026-06-13 hellenicshippingnews... Suezmax rates on USGC-to-Europe fell 22% m-o-m but up 130% y-o-y. Aframax rates on Mediterranean-to-Northwest Europe fell 51% m-o-m.

Increased long-haul demand supported VLCC spot freight rates. Reduced prompt buying following strong levels in previous month.

articles.title: Trade stands at a crossroads
articles.summary: The OECD has laid out two starkly different economic futures for global trade in its latest Economic Outlook. The report’s scenario analysis makes clear that the trajectory of Middle East-related disruptions to energy flows will determine not only growth, but the structure and resilience of global trade itself. At the centre of the OECD’s outlook ...
articles.body: Trade stands at a crossroads | Hellenic Shipping News Worldwide Recent News Why the economics make this the craziest World Cup ever Dangote ramp-up slashes WAF product imports, reshapes clean tanker routes: analysts Tanker Market Stays Elevated in May Trade stands at a crossroads Retrofitting at scale: Lessons from delivering the world’s first methanol mega containership conversion Trade stands at a crossroads in International Shipping News 13/06/2026 T he OECD has laid out two starkly different economic futures for global trade in its latest Economic Outlook. The report’s scenario analysis makes clear that the trajectory of Middle East-related disruptions to energy flows will determine not only growth, but the structure and resilience of global trade itself. At the centre of the OECD’s outlook lies a divergence. On one side is a “time-limited disruption scenario” in which supply chains gradually normalise. On the other is a “prolonged disruption scenario” that implies persistent supply shocks, structural damage to trade networks and a reordering of global commerce. Even in the more optimistic case, global trade is expected to lose momentum. The OECD projects that “global trade growth is projected to moderate from 5.0% in 2025 to 3.1% in 2026, and slow further to 2.9% in 2027”. This slowdown reflects both direct and indirect effects of energy disruption. Maritime chokepoints and elevated freight costs have already begun to weigh on flows, particularly between Asia, Europe and the Middle East. The report notes that trade weakness will be driven by “a sharp decline in trade with the Gulf economies and increasing energy and transport costs”. These factors illustrate how input shocks, rather than demand contraction alone, are now driving trade outcomes. However, offsets remain. Lower tariff rates in the US and continued demand for AI-related goods are expected to “help to sustain overall global growth”. This highlights a bifurcation within trade itself: while commodity-linked and energy-intensive flows weaken, technology-driven trade continues to provide momentum. Scenario divergence The OECD’s two scenarios hinge on the duration of supply disruptions emanating from the Gulf. In the baseline case, energy production recovers from mid-2026, allowing shipping routes and production networks to gradually stabilise. Under this pathway, shortages remain “limited and short lived”, and global trade adjusts rather than contracts structurally. By contrast, the prolonged disruption scenario represents a fundamentally different trade environment. The report assumes energy prices remain “50% higher” for an extended period, accompanied by “rising global supply shortages that cause disruptions to production in importing and exporting countries”. For global trade, this translates into both supply-side rationing and systemic inefficiencies. Indeed, the OECD warns that shortages of critical inputs would “result in an enforced rationing of energy for businesses”, combined with weaker confidence and tighter financial conditions. These conditions would compress both supply and demand for traded goods, producing what trade economists recognise as a two-sided shock. One of the most significant implications for global trade lies in the deepening fragmentation of supply chains. The OECD highlights how disruptions to key inputs—especially those concentrated in the Gulf—cascade through global production networks. The report notes that the region is a major exporter not only of hydrocarbons but also of industrial inputs such as fertilisers, petrochemicals and gases. As a result, “shortages begin to impact across a range of supply chains, as substitutes are not readily available for many products.” Semiconductor production, agriculture and chemicals all rely on inputs produced or processed in the region. In a prolonged disruption scenario, these sectors face compounding effects: input scarcity, higher costs and delayed production cycles. Moreover, indirect dependencies amplify the shock. The OECD emphasises that “indirect linkages via supply chains account for a large share of total exposure and often exceed direct dependence.” This means even economies with limited direct trade links to the Gulf are vulnerable through intermediate goods trade. Trade geography shifts under pressure The scenarios also point to a reshaping of global trade geography. In the time-limited case, Asian economies experience the sharpest short-term disruption but recover alongside energy flows. Strong export growth continues in dynamic Asian economies, particularly in technology sectors. However, in the prolonged scenario, these same economies become the epicentre of trade contraction. The OECD notes that “many economies in Asia are likely to be hit heavily, reflecting their relatively high reliance on energy inputs from the Gulf economies”. This is significant given Asia’s central position in manufacturing supply chains. At the same time, energy exporters could see temporary gains through improved terms of trade, though these are constrained if production itself is disrupted. Meanwhile, import-dependent emerging markets face deteriorating balances and rising financing costs, potentially limiting their participation in global trade. The OECD scenarios highlight longer-term structural shifts in trade policy. The vulnerability of supply chains to a single geographic chokepoint has underscored the need for diversification. The report calls for efforts to “strengthen the resilience of supply chains” and reduce dependency on concentrated sources of supply. This has direct implications for trade agreements and industrial policy. Governments are likely to prioritise supply chain security, potentially reshaping sourcing strategies, investment flows and trade partnerships. The emphasis on resilience may accelerate trends towards regionalisation and “friend-shoring” of critical industries. At the same time, the OECD stresses the importance of maintaining openness. It cautions that export restrictions and protectionist measures “only exacerbate global product shortages and push up prices”. For trade policymakers, this presents a delicate balancing act between resilience and openness. Ultimately, the OECD’s scenarios suggest that global trade is entering a phase defined less by tariffs and more by supply risk, energy security and geopolitical uncertainty. As the report concludes, “growth prospects depend on many factors, including the degree of damage to energy infrastructure… [and] the time it takes for normal trade patterns… to resume.” For the global trading system, those timelines will determine whether 2026 marks a temporary disruption—or the start of a more structural transformation. Source: OECD, Baltic Exchange Tops 2026-06-13 hellenicshippingnews... articles.title: Retrofitting at scale: Lessons from delivering the world’s first methanol mega containership conversion
articles.summary: The retrofitting of a 20,000 TEU container vessel to run on methanol was successfully completed in China in 2025. With valuable lessons already learned, this can pave the way for further conversion of the global fleet to low-GHG fuels, supporting wider decarbonization efforts. The world’s first methanol conversion project for a mega container carrier was ...
articles.body: Retrofitting at scale: Lessons from delivering the world’s first methanol mega containership conversion | Hellenic Shipping News Worldwide Recent News Why the economics make this the craziest World Cup ever Dangote ramp-up slashes WAF product imports, reshapes clean tanker routes: analysts Tanker Market Stays Elevated in May Trade stands at a crossroads Retrofitting at scale: Lessons from delivering the world’s first methanol mega containership conversion Retrofitting at scale: Lessons from delivering the world’s first methanol mega containership conversion in International Shipping News 13/06/2026 The retrofitting of a 20,000 TEU container vessel to run on methanol was successfully completed in China in 2025. With valuable lessons already learned, this can pave the way for further conversion of the global fleet to low-GHG fuels, supporting wider decarbonization efforts. The world’s first methanol conversion project for a mega container carrier was completed at CHI‑Shanghai’s yard in September 2025. At nearly 400 metres in length and with a capacity of 20,000 TEU, the seven‑year‑old vessel became the first ship of its kind to be retrofitted to run on methanol. Delivered through close coordination between COSCO Shipping, CHI Shanghai, designers, suppliers, and DNV, and supported by a process of continuous learning, the project demonstrated that deep, first‑of‑a‑kind retrofits can be executed safely, on time, and at industrial scale. Designing a methanol conversion for a megaship The vessel’s fuel system, designed only for conventional fuels, required fundamental changes to enable safe and compliant methanol operation across propulsion, auxiliaries, storage, and safety systems. The general design was developed by MARIC, while CHI Shanghai carried out the detailed engineering and served as EPC contractor. The scope included conversion of the ship’s MAN B&W 11S90 main engine and two of its four Wärtsilä auxiliary engines to dual‑fuel operation. In parallel, new methanol fuel tanks with a total capacity exceeding 15,000 cubic metres were installed forward of the engine room, together with new fuel preparation and supply systems. Coordinating the complexity of retrofitting Delivering this as a retrofit rather than a newbuild added complexity that is often underestimated. Existing structural arrangements could not simply be replaced, and new systems had to be integrated into confined spaces, requiring innovative construction sequences and tight interface management between suppliers. “This was a very complex project involving many parties, including engine makers, fuel system suppliers, and automation and safety specialists,” says Yan Hao, Commercial Director at CHI‑Shanghai. “It was also the first time all of these suppliers had worked together on a methanol retrofit of this scale. Coordination was critical.” Why phased construction proved more efficient One of the earliest technical decisions illustrated how established assumptions had to be challenged. The original plan envisaged fabricating the methanol fuel tank and preparation room as a single integrated block, to be lifted and installed on board. In practice, this proved impractical due to extensive high-altitude work and its impact on yard productivity. Instead, the yard adopted a phased construction strategy. “This involved assembling major components at ground level before integrating them into the vessel,” says Yan. “The change improved safety, reduced installation risk, and ultimately proved more efficient.” Applying first vessel lessons cuts methanol retrofit yard time by weeks Commissioning posed even a greater challenge. Converting engines to methanol operation is not simply a mechanical exercise. Control systems, automation layers, and remote-operation interfaces must function seamlessly under operating conditions that differ significantly from conventional fuels. “For the first vessel, software commissioning took far longer than expected,” Yan explains. “Because this was a fist-of-its-kind retrofit involving several suppliers, there was no baseline data. The integration of multiple systems required extensive testing and recalibration.” The result was a total yard stay of 108 days for COSCO Shipping Libra, including eight days of sea trials. When the sister vessel COSCO Shipping Gemini entered the yard later the same year, the same core team was able to apply lessons learnt from the first project. This resulted in completing the retrofit in just 90 days, demonstrating clear efficiency gains. Early class engagement underpins safe and compliant methanol retrofits Alongside the physical work in the yard, the project was shaped by extensive preparatory efforts well before the vessel arrived in drydock. Given the novelty and scale of the conversion, close engagement with class was essential to ensure that safety, compliance, and operability were addressed alongside engineering needs. DNV supported the project through early design reviews and, system-integration discussions and approval processes, working closely with the owner, yard, and suppliers. This included building methanol‑specific competence within the local survey team well in advance of the conversion. “For a project like this, early preparation is essential,” says Boris Bondarenko, Senior Vice President and Area Manager China FiS Maritime, at DNV. “This means assessing how entirely new fuel systems, engines, automation, and safety concepts interact on an existing vessel.” Throughout retrofit and sea trials, class oversight focused on the integration of methanol systems into the vessel’s architecture and the management of operational and safety risks. The experience has since informed subsequent methanol and LNG retrofit projects. The owner’s perspective: From concept to operation For COSCO Shipping, the decision to pursue a methanol retrofit was both strategic and practical. While newbuilds remain central to fleet renewal, the company recognized that existing tonnage must also be addressed to meet decarbonization targets. “Retrofitting allows us to act now,” says Zhang Jun, Safety Supervision Manager, Technology & Innovation Department, COSCO Shipping Lines, Co., Ltd. “But it also comes with trade‑offs that have to be understood and managed.” One visible consequence was reduced container capacity. To accommodate methanol tanks and systems COSCO Shipping Libra lost 456 TEU, requiring careful reassessment of deployment and network planning. Fuel availability and handling were also key considerations. While methanol infrastructure is developing rapidly on major trade lanes, it remains less mature than conventional fuels. Operational teams had to adapt to new bunkering procedures, safety protocols, and fuel‑management practices. “At the operational level, the learning curve is real,” Jun explains. “Crew training, maintenance planning, and fuel logistics all have to evolve together.” Early operational experience Since re‑entering service, COSCO SHIPPING Libra has demonstrated stable operation on methanol, giving COSCO confidence in the technical solution. Dual‑fuel capability allows the vessel to switch fuels as required, offering flexibility during early deployment. “The project showed that even very large, modern container ships can be converted successfully,” Jun says. “That changes how we think about the future of the fleet.” At the same time, the company remains realistic about limitations. Methanol fuel injectors and certain sealing systems still face durability challenges, and long-term lifecycle data is still emerging, reinforcing the need for further close collaboration with yards, suppliers, and class. Scaling retrofit capability in China From the yard’s perspective, the COSCO project has become a reference point for managing complexity at scale. CHI Shanghai has since applied similar methodologies to other projects, including multi‑vessel methanol retrofits for Seaspan and potential LNG retrofits f
